name: Yata Mirror
description: The Yata Mirror (八咫鏡, Yata no Kagami) is one of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan, symbolizing wisdom or honesty, depending on the interpretation. Forged by the smith Ishikoridome, it played a crucial role in luring Amaterasu, the sun goddess, out of the Ama-no-Iwato (Rock Cave of Heaven). When Amaterasu, distraught by her brother Susanoo's behavior, hid in the cave, plunging the world into darkness, the other gods devised a plan. They crafted the Yata Mirror and adorned a sakaki tree with it and other jewels. Ame-no-Uzume, the goddess of mirth, performed a provocative dance, causing the other gods to erupt in laughter. Drawn by the noise, Amaterasu peeked out of the cave, and upon seeing her reflection in the Yata Mirror, she was captivated by its brilliance and emerged, restoring light to the world. The Yata Mirror's reflective surface is believed to reveal the true nature of things, exposing lies and deception. It represents truth and sincerity, acting as a symbol of self-reflection and introspection. The mirror is currently enshrined in Ise Grand Shrine, one of the most sacred Shinto shrines in Japan, although it is never publicly displayed. Its presence is a constant reminder of the importance of honesty and wisdom in leadership and governance. It is an important symbol of the Japanese Imperial family and their connection to the divine.
subtitle: Symbol of wisdom and honesty.
material: Metal (believed to be bronze or copper)
origin: Forged by the smith Ishikoridome to lure Amaterasu from the cave.
currentLocation: Ise Grand Shrine, Japan (believed to be)

keyMyths.title: Amaterasu and the Cave
keyMyths.description: The Yata Mirror was used to lure Amaterasu out of the Ama-no-Iwato, restoring light to the world. The mirror's reflection captivated the sun goddess, drawing her out of hiding.

keyMyths.title: Imperial Regalia
keyMyths.description: The Yata Mirror is one of the Three Sacred Treasures, symbolizing the emperor's wisdom and connection to the divine. Its presence signifies the legitimacy and authority of the Imperial line.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: English translation of the Kojiki, Japan's oldest historical record.
sources.text: English translation of the Kojiki, Japan's oldest historical record.

sources.author: W.G. Aston
sources.description: English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another important historical text.
sources.text: English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another important historical text.

sources.author: Richard Ponsonby-Fane
sources.description: A detailed exploration of Shinto beliefs and the significance of shrines.
sources.text: A detailed exploration of Shinto beliefs and the significance of shrines.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings at Ise Grand Shrine
worshipPractices.description: The Yata Mirror is enshrined in the Ise Grand Shrine, specifically the Inner Shrine (Naiku), dedicated to Amaterasu. Devotees offer prayers and gifts to Amaterasu, indirectly honoring the mirror as a sacred object representing her divine presence. These offerings can include food, textiles, and other valuable items.

worshipPractices.name: Purification Rituals
worshipPractices.description: Mirrors, including the Yata Mirror (symbolically), are often associated with purification in Shinto. Worshippers may use mirrors in rituals to reflect and dispel negative energies, seeking spiritual cleansing and renewal. This practice connects to the mirror's role in revealing Amaterasu's radiant light, banishing darkness.

festivals.name: Shikinen Sengu (Rebuilding of Ise Shrine)
festivals.description: Every twenty years, the Ise Grand Shrine, including the Naiku where the Yata Mirror is enshrined, is completely rebuilt. This elaborate ritual symbolizes renewal and the continuation of tradition, reaffirming the sacredness of the mirror and its connection to the imperial lineage and national identity.

festivals.name: New Year's Celebrations
festivals.description: During New Year's, many Japanese people visit shrines, including Ise Grand Shrine if possible, to pray for good fortune and well-being. The Yata Mirror, as a symbol of honesty and wisdom, is indirectly honored as people reflect on the past year and seek guidance for the future, aligning with the mirror's symbolic representation.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Yata Mirror, as one of the Three Sacred Treasures, remains a potent symbol of imperial authority and national identity. It frequently appears in artistic representations of Japanese mythology and continues to inspire themes of honesty, wisdom, and the power of light in contemporary art and literature. The mirror's association with self-reflection also makes it a recurring motif in modern media exploring themes of identity and truth.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Athena's shield, often bearing the Gorgon's head, reflects the truth and can be used to reveal hidden dangers or enemies, similar to the Yata Mirror's association with honesty and wisdom.
Mimir's Well, guarded by Mimir, grants wisdom and knowledge to those who drink from it, resonating with the Yata Mirror's symbolic representation of wisdom and enlightenment.
The Eye of Horus symbolizes protection, healing, and restoration, often associated with truth and understanding, mirroring the Yata Mirror's capacity to bring clarity and reveal hidden truths.

associations.name: Truth
associations.significance: The mirror reflects reality without distortion, representing the pursuit and revelation of truth.

associations.name: Self-reflection
associations.significance: Mirrors encourage introspection and understanding of one's own nature, promoting self-awareness.

associations.name: Light
associations.significance: As a reflector, the mirror captures and redirects light, symbolizing enlightenment and clarity.

associations.name: Silver
associations.significance: The metallic surface of the mirror is often silver, a color associated with purity and reflection.

associations.name: Sun
associations.significance: The mirror lured Amaterasu (sun goddess) out of hiding, forging a strong symbolic connection to the sun.

associations.name: Wisdom
associations.significance: The Yata Mirror is often interpreted as a symbol of wisdom, representing clear perception and understanding.

associations.name: Eight
associations.significance: The name 'Yata' (八咫) means 'eight spans', a number often associated with vastness and completeness.

associations.name: Honesty
associations.significance: The mirror's ability to show things as they are links it to the virtue of honesty and integrity.

companions.name: Amaterasu
companions.description: The sun goddess, Amaterasu, is the one who ultimately benefits from the Yata Mirror's use, as it lures her out of the cave and restores light to the world. The mirror's power is intrinsically linked to her divine presence.

companions.name: Ishikoridome
companions.description: Ishikoridome is the divine smith who forged the Yata Mirror. Without her skill and craftsmanship, the mirror wouldn't exist and couldn't serve its purpose.

companions.name: Ame-no-Uzume
companions.description: Ame-no-Uzume performed a provocative dance that, combined with the Yata Mirror's reflection, intrigued and lured Amaterasu from the cave. Her performance was crucial to the mirror's effectiveness.

companions.name: Futodama
companions.description: Futodama held the Yata Mirror, displaying it prominently before the entrance of Ama-no-Iwato. He played a key role in the setup that lured Amaterasu out.

extendedContent.title: Creation & Forging of the Yata Mirror
extendedContent.content:
The creation of the Yata Mirror is intrinsically linked to the narrative of Amaterasu's retreat into the Ama-no-Iwato. According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, when the sun goddess Amaterasu hid in the Rock Cave of Heaven, the other deities orchestrated a plan to entice her out. This involved a grand celebration and the creation of objects designed to pique her curiosity. It was the smith god Ishikoridome who was tasked with forging the Yata no Kagami. The name 'Yata' refers to its size; 'yata' is an ancient Japanese unit of measurement, roughly equivalent to a large span, indicating that the mirror was unusually large for its time. 

Details regarding the forging process itself are sparse in the primary sources, but the significance lies in Ishikoridome's divine craftsmanship. As the deity of metallurgy and metalworking, Ishikoridome possessed the skill and knowledge necessary to create a mirror of exceptional quality. The mirror wasn't merely functional; it was intended to be a divine artifact, imbued with spiritual power. The act of creating it was a sacred undertaking, performed with the specific intention of restoring light and order to the world.

The successful forging of the Yata Mirror can be interpreted as a testament to the power of craftsmanship and ingenuity in overcoming adversity. It highlights the importance of skilled artisans and their ability to create objects of both practical and spiritual value, capable of influencing even the most powerful deities. The creation of the mirror is not just a technical achievement, but a vital component of a larger cosmological event, demonstrating the interconnectedness of the divine and human realms.

extendedContent.title: Powers & Properties: Reflecting Truth and Divinity
extendedContent.content:
The Yata Mirror's primary power is its ability to reflect not just physical images, but also inner truths and the divine essence of Amaterasu. It acts as a focal point, capturing and amplifying the goddess's radiance. When Amaterasu peeked out of the Ama-no-Iwato and saw her own reflection in the mirror, she was captivated by its brilliance and beauty, which ultimately led to her emerging from the cave. This demonstrates the mirror's power to reveal and attract the divine.

Beyond its literal reflective properties, the Yata Mirror is often interpreted as a symbol of honesty and self-reflection. It encourages those who gaze upon it to examine their own hearts and actions. This symbolic interpretation aligns with Shinto values of purity and sincerity. The mirror serves as a constant reminder to live an upright and truthful life. Some scholars also suggest the mirror embodies wisdom, as true understanding often requires honest self-assessment.

The mirror's presence among the Three Sacred Treasures reinforces its sacred status. It is not simply an object; it is a conduit to the divine, possessing the power to influence and guide. Its enduring significance in Japanese culture stems from its association with Amaterasu and its representation of fundamental values like truth, honesty, and self-awareness. The mirror's power, therefore, extends beyond the mythical realm, impacting moral and ethical considerations in the mortal world.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism: Wisdom, Honesty, and Self-Reflection
extendedContent.content:
The Yata Mirror is rich in symbolism, representing key concepts in Japanese culture and Shinto beliefs. Its association with Amaterasu, the sun goddess, inherently links it to light, truth, and enlightenment. Just as the sun illuminates the world, the mirror reflects reality, exposing falsehoods and revealing the truth. This connection to truth is a central aspect of its symbolic meaning, making it a potent emblem of honesty and integrity.

Furthermore, the mirror's reflective surface serves as a metaphor for self-reflection. By looking into the mirror, one is encouraged to confront their own imperfections and strive for self-improvement. This emphasis on self-awareness aligns with the Shinto emphasis on purity and moral rectitude. The mirror acts as a constant reminder to examine one's thoughts, words, and actions, promoting a life of honesty and sincerity. It is not merely a tool for vanity, but a spiritual instrument for introspection and growth.

The Yata Mirror's symbolic weight extends to its role in the imperial regalia. As one of the Three Sacred Treasures, it represents the emperor's legitimacy and connection to the divine lineage of Amaterasu. Its presence in imperial ceremonies reinforces the idea that the emperor should embody the virtues of wisdom, honesty, and justice. In this context, the mirror serves as a constant reminder of the responsibilities and moral obligations associated with leadership.

extendedContent.title: The Yata Mirror Today: Preservation and Reverence
extendedContent.content:
The Yata Mirror is currently enshrined in the Ise Grand Shrine, specifically in the Inner Shrine (Naiku), dedicated to Amaterasu. Its exact appearance remains a closely guarded secret, known only to the highest-ranking priests and priestesses of the shrine. Due to its sacred nature and its role as a symbol of imperial authority, the Yata Mirror itself is never displayed publicly. Instead, a replica is used in ceremonies and rituals.

The preservation of the Yata Mirror is of utmost importance, reflecting the deep respect for tradition and the enduring significance of this artifact. The Ise Grand Shrine is rebuilt every twenty years in a ritual called Shikinen Sengu, ensuring the continuity of the shrine and the proper maintenance of its sacred objects. This elaborate rebuilding process symbolizes renewal and purification, further emphasizing the reverence accorded to the Yata Mirror.

Despite its inaccessibility to the general public, the Yata Mirror continues to hold a powerful symbolic presence in Japanese culture. It is a reminder of the nation's rich mythological heritage and the enduring values of truth, honesty, and self-reflection. Its association with Amaterasu and the imperial lineage ensures its continued importance as a symbol of national identity and spiritual significance.

shortDescription: A sacred mirror, the Yata no Kagami lured Amaterasu from her cave, restoring light to the world. It symbolizes wisdom and honesty, and is one of Japan's Three Sacred Treasures.

wielders.name: Amaterasu
wielders.context: Intended recipient of the mirror's reflection to draw her out of the cave

wielders.name: Unknown Priestesses
wielders.context: Traditional Keepers of the Mirror at Ise Grand Shrine
